records.note: From teacher of online workshop at Creative Non Fiction magazine.Dear Thomas,Firstly, this is a very funny and promising first draft, ripe with potential. I particularly liked the concept of creating a guide for parents of potential writers. At the sentence-level, your prose is glass-clear and economical and works well to deliver those punch lines. The faux-scientific/manifesto-ish diction worked well too. At this point, while the piece is funny, I ultimately think we need to kick this thing into an even more exaggerated state and really aim for hyperbole. With a piece like this, the more outrageous the better.For the revision, I wonder if you might let yourself write even more specific prescriptions for what parents of potential writers should do. Perhaps you could invent a daily writing regimen. Perhaps you could develop a reading list for types of writers. “If you want your son or daughter to become a beret-doffing literati, someone who wears a Trotsky mustache and invariably smells like fennel, then have him or her read David Foster Wallace, John Barth, and Mark Leyner.” Or something like that. Also, too, because the “we” voice felt a little forced to me, I wonder whether you could instead offer a different subtext—the idea that you’re writing this guide because your parents didn’t do this and you thus resent them (obviously this would invoke a jokey tone). It’s something to consider and would maybe help with further exaggerated the tone of the piece.All things considered, though, this is really solid and funny work. It’s been a pleasure to read your work throughout the term, and I wish you all the best of luck as you complete your MFA. Do let me know if you’ve any questions or concerns. All best,Barrett

On Aug 29, 2012, at 6:36 PM, Editor wrote:Dear Thomas,Thank you very much for your submission of "How Robert Frost Helped Me Talk to My Father." Unfortunately, we have decided not to publish it.Personal note from the editor:This story is not badly written, but it's not quite what I'm looking for. I'm after stories with more plot and action than this. (Not necessarily car chases, but just more things happening.) Flash fiction really needs to grab the reader from the very beginning and be interesting enough to hold their attention all the way through.Best wishes,Jean MartinEditor, Free Flash Fiction@freeflashfichttp://www.freeflashfiction.com/If you use Duotrope, please consider reporting our response time:https://duotrope.com/market_8214.aspx

Dear Thomas, Thank you for submitting Robert Frost Helps Me Talk to My Father to Bartleby Snopes. We appreciate the chance to read your work. Unfortunately, this piece is not for us. We tend to prefer stories with more dramatic tension and more fully developed characters. This is more of anecdote. It needs more substance to carry the narrative, such as more demonstrated tension and conflict between the father and son and clarification about their relationship. Good luck with this one elsewhere. Feel free to try us again in the future with something new, but please wait at least one month before submitting again. Sincerely, The Editors Bartleby Snopes www.bartlebysnopes.comYou can go here to view the submission:http://bartlebysnopes.submittable.com/user/submissions/3860190

From teacher of online workshop at Creative Non Fiction magazine.Dear Thomas,Firstly, this is a very funny and promising first draft, ripe with potential. I particularly liked the concept of creating a guide for parents of potential writers. At the sentence-level, your prose is glass-clear and economical and works well to deliver those punch lines. The faux-scientific/manifesto-ish diction worked well too. At this point, while the piece is funny, I ultimately think we need to kick this thing into an even more exaggerated state and really aim for hyperbole. With a piece like this, the more outrageous the better.For the revision, I wonder if you might let yourself write even more specific prescriptions for what parents of potential writers should do. Perhaps you could invent a daily writing regimen. Perhaps you could develop a reading list for types of writers. “If you want your son or daughter to become a beret-doffing literati, someone who wears a Trotsky mustache and invariably smells like fennel, then have him or her read David Foster Wallace, John Barth, and Mark Leyner.” Or something like that. Also, too, because the “we” voice felt a little forced to me, I wonder whether you could instead offer a different subtext—the idea that you’re writing this guide because your parents didn’t do this and you thus resent them (obviously this would invoke a jokey tone). It’s something to consider and would maybe help with further exaggerated the tone of the piece.All things considered, though, this is really solid and funny work. It’s been a pleasure to read your work throughout the term, and I wish you all the best of luck as you complete your MFA. Do let me know if you’ve any questions or concerns. All best,Barrett

The first two poems, "Rat" and "With Father," areprose poems and, if accepted, might appear better if printed using a full-justify alignment(I don't know how to full-justify in an email). None of these poems has been submitted elsewherebut I may do so at a later date. I will certainly inform you is any of them is accepted elsewhere.RatMy mind is a rat, scuttering through a corn crib, looking for something to eat. Over there, maybethere’s something over there. I’m hungry, hungry. My toenails scrape on the rough woodplanking of the world. Something rotting. I turn away. For thirty years, my little rat mind turnsaway, pretends this place does not exist. This fifteen seconds—fifteen seconds between thetime my baby brother's front tire reached out and grabbed a ditch and I fell deeply intodisrepair. Behind the walls. It's impossible to see. There is nothing whatsoever to eat.With FatherI went deer hunting one weekend with my heavyset father, all black-rimmed glasses and buzzcut. Me, my father and two rifles. Before we left, I spent an overcast afternoon with mymother in thigh-high waterproof boots, showing her the slow running creek where my muskrattraps were planted. I drew her a paper map, x’s marking the spots. I asked her to get up earlyin the morning to clear the pelts. I handed her a red monkey wrench. At thirteen, I had adifficult time explaining the wrench. I was worried that if she knew muskrats bit she would sayno. I told her to shut her eyes, and wield it quickly, and everything would be okay. She saidthat was true of many things. CatfishI don’t know what turbulencehe encountered on the airplane ride,from Bumbleshit, Arkansas back homeafter his eldest son telephonedto say his baby Jimmy was goneand the airline paid for his flight.I don’t know who held him underhis armpits to keep him from falling to the ground when he first heard.But the weight of the news on his shoulder had pushed him so far into the past, whenhe walked (no, shuffled) up the gangplankinto the harsh sunlight of terminal B,where his surviving sons waited,my two brothers and I, he lookedlike the youngest of us all,small and frail, and no longerthe great-granddaddy catfishthat I remembered him to be.BioThomas Jay Rush is the owner of a small internet-based software company, a fact he chooses to ignore,focusing instead on writing short fiction, creative non-fiction, and poetry.  Jay lives with his familyin Gladwyne, Pennsylvania.=================================Thomas Jay RushGreat Hill Corporation(800) 949-3334jrush@greathill.com

We think the three poets we've had the pleasure of featuring all know how to make a damn good sandwich, different in their own way, but with a common attraction to every day life and its whimsy, sometimes tragedy, despair, hope. They've set out on the journey to, in the words of Raymond Carver, "endow those things--a chair, a window curtain, a fork, a stone, a woman's earring--with immense, even startling power."We came upon one of Thomas Jay Rush's short stories in which the unlikely word "peas" (empowered by just the right punctuation) gave this startling effect. His poems deal similarly with everyday things and feelings in a language that is non-ornamental but that still (maybe because of this) direct our attention elsewhere, outside the poem, toward some mystery or other.http://kitchenpoet.blogspot.com/2011_01_01_archive.html

From Paul Martin at Philadelphia Writer's Conference:Thomas, I've read your poem carefully, noticing, at the top of the page, your identifying it as a humorous poem. The last stanza of the poem suggests that the idea at the heart of the poem was one of those ideas that comes to someone who's had a few too many jars. Whether you take the humorous or a serious approach (earth as living organism), you'd make a stronger poem of you spoke in sharp images rather than in abstractions. Your first and second stanzas contain your most concrete language: "through the ground," "Through the soles/of our feet, up our legs,/ and deep into our hearts." That's where the poem is most effective, and it's because of the imagery. Compare those passage to the abstract language of the rest of the poem, which tells, rather than shows. Poetry enable us to see, hear, taste, touch, smell, and it does that, among other ways, with imagery. If you want to talk about this, let me know. There may be a serious poem in the idea you argue about, not a "giant, breathing brain," but a living earth that expresses itself to people, close to the natural world, people who live in that world and mystics, as well.

We think the three poets we've had the pleasure of featuring all know how to make a damn good sandwich, different in their own way, but with a common attraction to every day life and its whimsy, sometimes tragedy, despair, hope. They've set out on the journey to, in the words of Raymond Carver, "endow those things--a chair, a window curtain, a fork, a stone, a woman's earring--with immense, even startling power."We came upon one of Thomas Jay Rush's short stories in which the unlikely word "peas" (empowered by just the right punctuation) gave this startling effect. His poems deal similarly with everyday things and feelings in a language that is non-ornamental but that still (maybe because of this) direct our attention elsewhere, outside the poem, toward some mystery or other.http://kitchenpoet.blogspot.com/2011_01_01_archive.html

The first two poems, "Rat" and "With Father," areprose poems and, if accepted, might appear better if printed using a full-justify alignment(I don't know how to full-justify in an email). None of these poems has been submitted elsewherebut I may do so at a later date. I will certainly inform you is any of them is accepted elsewhere.RatMy mind is a rat, scuttering through a corn crib, looking for something to eat. Over there, maybethere’s something over there. I’m hungry, hungry. My toenails scrape on the rough woodplanking of the world. Something rotting. I turn away. For thirty years, my little rat mind turnsaway, pretends this place does not exist. This fifteen seconds—fifteen seconds between thetime my baby brother's front tire reached out and grabbed a ditch and I fell deeply intodisrepair. Behind the walls. It's impossible to see. There is nothing whatsoever to eat.With FatherI went deer hunting one weekend with my heavyset father, all black-rimmed glasses and buzzcut. Me, my father and two rifles. Before we left, I spent an overcast afternoon with mymother in thigh-high waterproof boots, showing her the slow running creek where my muskrattraps were planted. I drew her a paper map, x’s marking the spots. I asked her to get up earlyin the morning to clear the pelts. I handed her a red monkey wrench. At thirteen, I had adifficult time explaining the wrench. I was worried that if she knew muskrats bit she would sayno. I told her to shut her eyes, and wield it quickly, and everything would be okay. She saidthat was true of many things. CatfishI don’t know what turbulencehe encountered on the airplane ride,from Bumbleshit, Arkansas back homeafter his eldest son telephonedto say his baby Jimmy was goneand the airline paid for his flight.I don’t know who held him underhis armpits to keep him from falling to the ground when he first heard.But the weight of the news on his shoulder had pushed him so far into the past, whenhe walked (no, shuffled) up the gangplankinto the harsh sunlight of terminal B,where his surviving sons waited,my two brothers and I, he lookedlike the youngest of us all,small and frail, and no longerthe great-granddaddy catfishthat I remembered him to be.BioThomas Jay Rush is the owner of a small internet-based software company, a fact he chooses to ignore,focusing instead on writing short fiction, creative non-fiction, and poetry.  Jay lives with his familyin Gladwyne, Pennsylvania.=================================Thomas Jay RushGreat Hill Corporation(800) 949-3334jrush@greathill.com

From Paul Martin at Philadelphia Writer's Conference:Thomas, I've read your poem carefully, noticing, at the top of the page, your identifying it as a humorous poem. The last stanza of the poem suggests that the idea at the heart of the poem was one of those ideas that comes to someone who's had a few too many jars. Whether you take the humorous or a serious approach (earth as living organism), you'd make a stronger poem of you spoke in sharp images rather than in abstractions. Your first and second stanzas contain your most concrete language: "through the ground," "Through the soles/of our feet, up our legs,/ and deep into our hearts." That's where the poem is most effective, and it's because of the imagery. Compare those passage to the abstract language of the rest of the poem, which tells, rather than shows. Poetry enable us to see, hear, taste, touch, smell, and it does that, among other ways, with imagery. If you want to talk about this, let me know. There may be a serious poem in the idea you argue about, not a "giant, breathing brain," but a living earth that expresses itself to people, close to the natural world, people who live in that world and mystics, as well.
